chiatura.heroTitle: Chiatura, Georgia: Soviet Cable Cars and the Katskhi Pillar
chiatura.content:
Chiatura is a mining town in the Imereti region of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — in a narrow canyon cut by the Kvirila River about 70 kilometers northeast of Kutaisi. Around 12,000 people live here, and it looks unlike almost anywhere else in Georgia: steep canyon walls on both sides of the river, Soviet apartment blocks and industrial facilities clinging to the slopes, and a network of cable cars strung between the canyon rim and the valley floor. It isn't a conventional tourist destination — no medieval old town, no wine culture, no beach. What it has is a raw, industrial, visually dramatic atmosphere that has made it a real point of interest for travelers looking beyond the standard Georgian itinerary.

Why visit Chiatura?

The main reasons to visit Chiatura are its Soviet-era urban cable cars, a near-unaltered Soviet industrial townscape, and the nearby Katskhi Pillar — a medieval church on top of a 40-meter limestone monolith. The cliffside Mghvimevi Monastery adds a medieval contrast to the industrial setting. This is a destination for travelers interested in Soviet history, unusual landscapes, and photography, not for classic sightseeing or comfort. Most people visit as a day trip from Kutaisi.

The canyon and the cable cars

Chiatura's geography created a transport problem from the start. The canyon is deep and steep-walled, the manganese deposits lie mainly in the hills above, and the town developed both on the rim and along the river below. Moving workers, equipment, and ore between those levels was a constant challenge, and the Soviet solution was ambitious: a comprehensive urban cable car network linking the valley floor to the neighborhoods and mining areas on the plateau.

Construction began in the 1950s and continued through the 1960s, eventually producing one of the most extensive urban cable car systems in the Soviet Union, with multiple lines. The original cabins were functional Soviet industrial equipment — small metal boxes built for utility, which earned the local nickname "metal coffins." For decades they were simply how Chiatura's residents got around, as ordinary as a bus.

The system fell into disrepair after the Soviet collapse, and most lines stopped during the 1990s. A restoration project in the 2010s brought several lines back with upgraded cabins and infrastructure, and they now work as both local transport and tourist attraction. Riding them is the central experience of a visit: the views over the canyon, the river, the industrial landscape, and the forested hills beyond are unlike anything else in Georgia. One practical caveat — schedules and operating hours can be irregular, so check locally before planning around them [NEEDS: confirm which cable car lines currently operate, their hours, and fares].

Soviet architecture and urban atmosphere

Beyond the cable cars, Chiatura offers something increasingly rare in Georgia and the wider post-Soviet world: a Soviet industrial townscape that hasn't been significantly modernized, demolished, or cosmetically tidied up. The concrete apartment blocks, the workers' cultural centers, the administrative buildings with their heavy Soviet decoration, and the mining infrastructure on the hillsides all remain largely as built — weathered and worn but intact. For travelers interested in 20th-century history, Soviet architecture, or urban landscapes that are vanishing fast elsewhere, Chiatura is a significant place.

This isn't a theme-park version of Soviet history. Chiatura is a functioning town where people live and work, and the Soviet buildings are simply the buildings that exist here, not preserved exhibits. That ordinariness is exactly what gives the place its power. Walking the residential districts on the canyon rim, or riding a cable car to a neighborhood that feels untouched by the past thirty years of Georgian change, gives a more immediate sense of the Soviet period than any museum could.

Katskhi Pillar

About 10 kilometers from Chiatura, and almost always part of a visit, the Katskhi Pillar is one of the most visually arresting sites in Georgia. It's a natural limestone monolith rising roughly 40 meters above the valley floor on a sheer, effectively unclimbable face. On its flat summit — barely large enough for a small building — sit the remains of a medieval church and hermitage, built and inhabited at various points by monks who made the ascent by rope ladder.

The pillar was largely inaccessible and little known outside the area for much of the 20th century. A mountaineering expedition in 1940 made the first documented modern ascent and confirmed the medieval structures at the top; later archaeology dated the church to roughly the 15th century, with earlier occupation likely. A monk named Maxime Qavtaradze famously lived at the summit in relative isolation for an extended period, which brought the pillar wider attention.

A metal ladder and climbing route now allows access to the summit, but it requires reasonable fitness and a tolerance for exposed heights — and access to the top is often restricted, particularly for women, since it's an active monastic site [NEEDS: confirm current summit-access rules and any restrictions]. The view from the base alone — the sheer rock face with the tiny church at the top — justifies the detour, and on clear days the surrounding forested valleys and limestone outcrops are exceptionally photogenic.

Mghvimevi Monastery

A short distance from the town center, carved into a cliff above the Kvirila River, Mghvimevi Monastery is a striking contrast to the industrial landscape around it. The monastery dates to the medieval period and works natural cave formations into its architecture — the main church occupies a large natural cavity in the rock, with built elements added around and in front. The site is active and well maintained, with a small monastic community in residence.

The approach involves a steep climb up stone steps cut into the cliff, rewarded with some of the better views over the river and canyon in the immediate area. The pairing of medieval religious architecture and dramatic natural setting — typical of Georgian monasteries, which consistently favored cliff faces and ridges — makes a sharp contrast with the Soviet town in the valley below.

Manganese and the making of the town

Chiatura exists because of geology. The hills around the Kvirila canyon hold some of the largest manganese deposits ever found, and once they were identified in the late 19th century the town's trajectory was set. Commercial mining began in earnest in the 1870s and expanded rapidly, drawing European capital — including the Nobel and Rothschild interests then developing the Baku oil fields — and workers from across the Caucasus. By the early 20th century Chiatura produced a substantial share of the world's manganese, a mineral essential to steelmaking and therefore strategically valuable.

The town also has a place in revolutionary history. In 1905, during the first Russian Revolution, Chiatura's miners established one of the most complete examples of Bolshevik control over a Georgian settlement, effectively running the town as a revolutionary commune for several months. The young Bolshevik organizer Joseph Stalin was directly involved and spent time here — a connection that later brought Chiatura particular attention and investment in the Soviet decades. Under Soviet rule the mining was nationalized and expanded to industrial scale, and the town was rebuilt with workers' housing, cultural institutions, and administrative buildings. The industry, though much reduced from its Soviet peak, still operates and remains central to the local economy.

How to get to Chiatura and get around

Chiatura is most easily reached from Kutaisi, about one and a half hours by road through the Imereti hills, with marshrutkas (minibuses) running between the two with reasonable frequency. From Tbilisi it's longer, around three hours, but the road passes through attractive countryside and can be broken along the way [NEEDS: confirm current marshrutka frequency Kutaisi–Chiatura]. Within town, the cable cars serve several neighborhoods and are the most interesting way to get around, though hours can be irregular. Taxis are available for Mghvimevi Monastery and the trip to Katskhi Pillar.

Accommodation is limited to small guesthouses, and Chiatura is most commonly visited as a day trip from Kutaisi. Staying overnight makes for a much quieter evening, which has its own appeal.

Best time to visit Chiatura

Spring and autumn offer the most comfortable conditions and the best light — a real consideration given how much of Chiatura's appeal is visual. The canyon and industrial landscape photograph particularly well in overcast conditions, when flat light emphasizes texture and form over color. Summer is warm and the hills are fully green, sharpening the contrast between the natural landscape and the industrial town. Winter brings occasional snow to the canyon, which transforms the atmosphere and produces some of the most dramatic conditions the town offers, though cable car operations may be reduced.

faq.title: What is Chiatura known for?
faq.content: Chiatura is known for its Soviet-era urban cable cars and an almost unaltered Soviet industrial townscape in a dramatic river canyon. It's also the base for the Katskhi Pillar, a medieval church atop a 40-meter limestone monolith, and the cliffside Mghvimevi Monastery.

faq.title: Can you ride the Chiatura cable cars?
faq.content: Yes. Several lines were restored in the 2010s and run as both local transport and a tourist attraction, with views over the canyon and industrial landscape. Hours and which lines operate can be irregular, so check locally before planning around them.

faq.title: What is the Katskhi Pillar?
faq.content: The Katskhi Pillar is a natural limestone monolith about 10 km from Chiatura, rising roughly 40 meters, with the remains of a medieval church and hermitage on its flat summit. The church dates to around the 15th century. The view from the base is dramatic; summit access is restricted as it's an active monastic site.

faq.title: How do you get to Chiatura?
faq.content: Most easily from Kutaisi, about one and a half hours by road with regular marshrutkas. From Tbilisi it's roughly three hours. Most people visit as a day trip from Kutaisi; accommodation in Chiatura is limited to small guesthouses.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Chiatura?
faq.content: Spring and autumn for comfortable conditions and good light. Overcast days actually suit the canyon and industrial scenery for photography. Summer sharpens the green-versus-industrial contrast; winter snow is dramatic but may reduce cable car operations.

dmanisi.heroTitle: Dmanisi, Georgia: The Oldest Human Ancestors Outside Africa
dmanisi.content:
Dmanisi is a small town in the Kvemo Kartli region of southern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — on a plateau at the meeting of the Mashavera and Pinezauri rivers, about 90 kilometers southwest of Tbilisi. Around 3,000 people live here, and the town itself is quiet and unhurried. What makes Dmanisi extraordinary is what lies beneath it: the oldest hominin remains ever found outside Africa, a discovery that changed the scientific understanding of when and how early humans first left their continent of origin. For that alone, it belongs on a very short list of places where the ground beneath your feet connects directly to the deepest questions about human history.

Why is Dmanisi important?

Dmanisi is important because hominin fossils found here, dating to roughly 1.8 million years ago, are the oldest evidence of early humans outside Africa. The find overturned the prevailing view that early humans only left Africa after developing large brains and sophisticated tools — the Dmanisi hominins were small-brained, used the most basic stone tools, and still reached the southern Caucasus. The same promontory also holds a fortified medieval town and a 6th-century cathedral, so a visit spans nearly two million years of human presence in one place.

The archaeological discovery

Excavations at Dmanisi have run since the 1930s, when the site was first investigated for its medieval remains. The transformative discoveries came later — from the 1980s through the 1990s and 2000s — when archaeologists digging beneath the medieval layers found fossil bones and stone tools dating to about 1.8 million years ago.

The fossils include five skulls and associated skeletal remains from early hominins who walked upright, used simple stone tools, and lived in a social group. They're generally classified as Homo erectus, though the variation between the five skulls is large enough that they were initially thought to be different species. A landmark 2013 study examining all five together concluded the variation falls within the natural range of a single species — a finding with major implications for how paleoanthropologists classify early human fossils from other sites worldwide.

Before Dmanisi, the consensus held that early humans didn't leave Africa until they had reached a certain threshold of brain size, body size, and tool sophistication thought necessary to survive elsewhere. The Dmanisi hominins overturned that. They were small-brained by later human standards, used simple Oldowan tools of the most basic type, and yet had traveled thousands of kilometers from Africa to the Caucasus. The implication is that human dispersal out of Africa began earlier, moved faster, and needed less technology than previously assumed.

One skull — the fourth recovered, belonging to an elderly individual — added an insight of a different kind. The individual had lost all but one tooth long before death, with significant bone resorption in the jaw, meaning they survived a long time unable to chew solid food. That suggests other members of the group provided care, possibly processing food for them — one of the earliest known examples of social care behavior in the hominin lineage. The stone tools found alongside the fossils are Oldowan: simple flakes and choppers made by striking one stone against another. Animal bones at the site show cut marks consistent with butchery, though whether the Dmanisi hominins were hunting or scavenging is still researched.

The site

The archaeological site occupies the same promontory as the medieval town, and the layering of time periods here is one of its most unusual features. The promontory is a natural defensive position — a peninsula of high ground defined on two sides by the deep ravines of the Mashavera and Pinezauri rivers, with a narrow neck connecting it to the plateau. That geography drew settlement across radically different eras, producing a stratigraphic record that runs from the 1.8-million-year-old volcanic ash layer with the hominin fossils, up through Bronze Age remains, early medieval occupation, and finally the fortified medieval city.

The Dmanisi Museum-Reserve manages both the prehistoric excavations and the medieval remains, and a visit takes in both. The museum building, opened in 2011, sits directly over part of the active excavation area and presents the fossil finds and their scientific context with original specimens and high-quality replicas. The exhibition is well designed and makes a serious effort to explain the significance to visitors without a paleoanthropology background. Casts of all five skulls are displayed together, so you can see the variation that made the 2013 study so significant [NEEDS: confirm Dmanisi Museum-Reserve opening hours, ticket price, and whether it closes seasonally].

Outside, the excavation trenches and medieval ruins are accessible on foot along marked paths. The medieval fortifications are substantial — stone walls, towers, and the remains of streets and buildings across the promontory — and the Dmanisi Sioni Cathedral, dating to the 6th century with later modifications, still stands largely intact at the center. It's one of the older surviving Christian churches in Georgia and would be a significant monument anywhere else; here it shares attention with far more ancient remains directly beneath its foundations.

The medieval town

The medieval settlement at Dmanisi peaked between the 9th and 13th centuries, when it was an important trading and administrative center on the routes linking the Caucasus with the Middle East and Central Asia. The town was fortified, commercially active, and large enough to support real architectural investment — the cathedral, the defensive walls, and the residential and commercial remains all date mainly to this period.

The town declined after the 13th-century Mongol invasions and the regional instability that followed, and by the 15th century it was largely abandoned. That abandonment is partly why the remains are so complete: a town that keeps being inhabited is continuously rebuilt, obscuring earlier layers, while one that is simply left largely keeps the form it had when abandoned.

The setting adds to the impact. The promontory drops steeply into the river ravines on two sides, and from the edges the views across the rolling, semi-arid, agricultural Kvemo Kartli landscape extend for a long way. The sense of isolation and exposure, combined with remains spanning nearly two million years, gives the site an atmosphere that's hard to replicate.

What else is nearby

Dmanisi sits within the broader Kvemo Kartli region, which has several other points of interest within a short drive. Bolnisi, with its 5th-century Sioni Cathedral carrying some of the earliest known examples of written Georgian script, is about 40 kilometers to the northeast. Rustavi, Georgia's second largest city and a center of Soviet-era heavy industry, lies further north. The semi-arid Kvemo Kartli plateau — unusual within Georgia's generally green, mountainous character — has its own austere appeal and is worth traveling slowly rather than just crossing on the way somewhere else.

How to get to Dmanisi

Dmanisi is about one and a half to two hours from Tbilisi by car, on the main road south through Kvemo Kartli. There's no direct marshrutka to the archaeological site itself: without your own transport, the practical options are a marshrutka to the town of Dmanisi plus a taxi for the final kilometers to the site, or an organized day tour from Tbilisi, which several operators run [NEEDS: confirm current marshrutka service to Dmanisi town and the Tbilisi departure point]. The drive passes through the open farmland of Kvemo Kartli and gives a good read on this less-visited part of southern Georgia.

Best time to visit Dmanisi

Spring and autumn are the most comfortable, with mild temperatures and clear skies that make walking the outdoor parts of the site pleasant. Summer can be genuinely hot on the open promontory, where shade is limited, so come in the morning if visiting in July or August. Winter is quiet and cool, occasionally bringing snow that transforms the landscape, and the low visitor numbers give the place a stillness that suits its character.

faq.title: Why is Dmanisi important?
faq.content: Dmanisi holds the oldest hominin remains ever found outside Africa, dating to roughly 1.8 million years ago. The discovery showed early humans left Africa earlier and with simpler tools and smaller brains than previously thought, reshaping the understanding of human dispersal.

faq.title: What was found at Dmanisi?
faq.content: Five early hominin skulls and associated skeletal remains, generally classified as Homo erectus, along with simple Oldowan stone tools and butchered animal bones. A 2013 study of all five skulls together concluded their variation falls within a single species.

faq.title: What is there to see at Dmanisi today?
faq.content: The Dmanisi Museum-Reserve, built over the excavation area, displays the fossil finds and casts of all five skulls. Outside, marked paths lead through the excavation trenches and the fortified medieval town, including the 6th-century Dmanisi Sioni Cathedral.

faq.title: How do you get to Dmanisi from Tbilisi?
faq.content: About one and a half to two hours by car. There's no direct marshrutka to the site itself, so most visitors without a car take a marshrutka to Dmanisi town plus a taxi, or join an organized day tour from Tbilisi.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Dmanisi?
faq.content: Spring and autumn for mild weather and clear skies. Summer can be hot on the exposed promontory with little shade, so visit in the morning. Winter is cool and quiet, occasionally with snow.

tbilisi.heroTitle: Tbilisi, Georgia: What to See, When to Go, and How Long to Stay
tbilisi.content:
Tbilisi is the capital of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus, not the US state — and its largest city, home to around 1.2 million people. It sits along the Mtkvari (Kura) River in a valley ringed by hills, which is why the city looks the way it does: houses and churches stacked up steep slopes, linked by staircases and twisting lanes rather than a grid. For most international travelers, Tbilisi is the entry point to Georgia and the natural base for trips to the wine country of Kakheti, the Greater Caucasus around Kazbegi (Stepantsminda), and the ancient town of Mtskheta.

The Old Town sits on the valley floor at around 380 meters; residential districts climb the surrounding hillsides to roughly 770 meters. That spread matters on foot — short distances on a map can involve real climbing.

Planning your visit? See our full guide to things to do in Tbilisi for specifics, timings, and local tips.

What is Tbilisi known for?

Tbilisi is known for a walkable historic core of carved wooden balconies, working sulfur bathhouses fed by natural hot springs, one of the world's oldest wine cultures, and a food scene that has drawn steady international attention over the past decade. The city carries about 1,500 years of layered history — Persian, Ottoman, Russian imperial, and Soviet influences sit side by side — which makes it unusually varied for a capital of its size.

How many days do you need in Tbilisi?

Most travelers spend two to three days in Tbilisi itself. Two days covers the Old Town, the sulfur baths, Narikala Fortress, Rustaveli Avenue, and a couple of museums at a reasonable pace. A third day leaves room for the food and wine scene, the newer neighborhoods, or a half-day at Mtskheta. If you plan to use Tbilisi as a base for day trips to Kakheti or Kazbegi, add a day for each.

A common mistake is rushing the city in a single day on the way to the mountains. You can do it, but you'll miss most of what makes Tbilisi worth the stop.

The Old Town (Dzveli Tbilisi)

The Old Town occupies the valley floor and lower slopes below Narikala Fortress on the right bank of the Mtkvari. It is the oldest continuously inhabited part of Tbilisi and the area that best reflects the city's pre-19th-century character.

Its signature feature is the traditional Tbilisi house with a carved wooden balcony — a style that developed over centuries and is found in this form almost nowhere else. Many balconies survive today, but restoration is uneven: some houses are beautifully restored, others are propped up and visibly crumbling. That contrast is part of how the Old Town actually looks, not a polished tourist set.

Narikala Fortress, on the ridge above, was first built in the 4th century and expanded under Arab and later Georgian rule. An earthquake in 1827 set off an explosion in a gunpowder store the Russian garrison kept inside the fortress, and Narikala was never fully rebuilt. What remains is a ruin rather than an intact castle, but its position makes it one of the best viewpoints in Tbilisi. A cable car connects the Old Town to the fortress and the park beside it.

At the base of the fortress is Abanotubani, the sulfur bath district. The domed brick bathhouses here are fed by natural hot springs and have operated in some form since the city's earliest days; the current buildings mostly date from the 17th to 19th centuries, and several still work as public baths. The water surfaces at roughly 37–47°C and is reheated for use. Historically these baths were where merchants met and deals were made — social spaces as much as places to wash.

Churches and cathedrals in Tbilisi

Few cities of Tbilisi's size have this density of significant religious buildings, and they span several traditions.

Sameba Cathedral — formally the Cathedral of the Holy Trinity — is the most prominent religious building in modern Tbilisi. Completed in 2004 after about a decade of construction, it stands on Elia Hill in the Avlabari district and is one of the largest Orthodox cathedrals in the world by floor area. Its golden dome rises about 84 meters [NEEDS: confirm 84 m dome height] and is visible across much of the city. The cathedral is the seat of the Catholicos-Patriarch of All Georgia.

Metekhi Church sits on a cliff above the Mtkvari at the eastern edge of the Old Town. The current church dates to the 13th century, rebuilt several times since. The equestrian statue of King Vakhtang Gorgasali beside it is one of the most photographed spots in Tbilisi.

Anchiskhati Basilica, in a quieter corner of the Old Town, is the oldest surviving church in Tbilisi, built in the 6th century during the reign of King Dachi. It is still an active parish church.

Sioni Cathedral served as the main cathedral of the Georgian Orthodox Church for centuries, and the Jumah Mosque has served Tbilisi's Muslim community since the 17th century — a reminder of how mixed the Old Town's population has long been.

The 19th-century city center: Rustaveli and Freedom Square

North of the Old Town, Tbilisi changes character. This district was largely built or rebuilt in the 19th century under Russian imperial rule, with wider streets, taller buildings, and a clear European influence.

Rustaveli Avenue is the main boulevard, running east to west through this district. Named after the 12th-century poet Shota Rustaveli, it is lined with the Georgian National Museum, the Rustaveli National Theatre, the Parliament building [NEEDS: confirm current use/status of the Rustaveli Parliament building], the Kashveti Church, and several historic hotels. It remains the city's main public space for major events.

Nearby Freedom Square — Lenin Square in the Soviet period — is the central civic plaza, with a gilded statue of St. George at its center where a Lenin statue once stood. Both Rustaveli and Freedom Square are functional civic spaces more than attractions in their own right; worth walking through, but not where you'll spend hours.

Neighborhoods worth knowing in Tbilisi

Beyond the Old Town and center, Tbilisi is a city of distinct districts.

Avlabari, on the left bank of the Mtkvari, has historically been the Armenian quarter and keeps several Armenian churches. Sameba Cathedral and its complex sit here.

Marjanishvili and Chugureti, west of the center, grew as mixed residential and commercial districts around 1900. Today they hold a dense run of cafés, restaurants, theaters, and independent shops in renovated historic buildings, and are among the liveliest parts of Tbilisi for going out.

Vera and Vake, further northwest, are mostly residential, developed through the Soviet era, and now hold many of the city's universities, embassies, and higher-end restaurants. Vake Park is one of the main green spaces for residents.

Museums and cultural institutions

The Georgian National Museum on Rustaveli Avenue is the single best place to understand Georgian history, with collections from prehistoric gold and medieval manuscripts to a Soviet occupation exhibition that gives essential 20th-century context. Within the same complex, the Simon Janashia Museum holds the Dmanisi hominid fossils — among the earliest human-ancestor remains found outside Africa.

The Museum of Fine Arts holds Georgian and Caucasian applied art, including the cloisonné enamelwork medieval Georgia was known for. The Open Air Museum of Ethnography, on the slopes of Mtatsminda, gathers traditional houses and farm buildings from across Georgia's regions on one hillside — a quick way to see how building styles differ between, say, Svaneti and the lowlands.

Georgian food in Tbilisi

Georgian cuisine has drawn real international attention recently, and Tbilisi is the best place to explore it in depth because the capital's restaurants cover every region. The food varies by area — Adjara on the Black Sea coast, the mountains of Svaneti and Tusheti, and the wine country of Kakheti all cook differently.

Khinkali, the large pleated dumplings filled with spiced meat and broth, are Georgia's most internationally recognizable dish. Khachapuri, cheese-filled bread, comes in regional forms; the Adjarian boat-shaped version with egg and butter is the richest. Lobiani is the same idea filled with spiced beans instead of cheese. Walnuts, pomegranate, tkemali (a tart plum sauce), and large amounts of fresh herbs run through the cuisine. Meat dishes lean toward pork and lamb, but the Orthodox fasting tradition also gives Georgia an unusually deep range of vegetable and bean dishes — useful to know if you're vegetarian.

Georgian wine in Tbilisi

Wine sits at the center of Georgian culture. Georgia is widely considered one of the oldest wine-producing regions in the world, with archaeological evidence of winemaking going back roughly 8,000 years. The traditional method ferments grapes — skins, seeds, and all — in large clay vessels called qvevri, sealed and buried in the ground. This produces the amber or "orange" wines Georgia is now known for, plus reds with a character quite unlike conventional European wine.

Georgia has more than 500 indigenous grape varieties, around 40 of them in commercial use. The main white is Rkatsiteli, grown mostly in Kakheti, with Mtsvane another important white; Saperavi is the dark-skinned grape behind the country's best-known reds. Tbilisi's wine bars often pour traditional qvevri styles and modern winery wines side by side, which makes the city a good place to taste before heading to the wineries themselves in Kakheti.

Getting around Tbilisi

Tbilisi has a two-line metro that covers the central corridor and several outlying districts — limited in reach but cheap and efficient for the center and major transit hubs. Buses and minibuses (marshrutkas) fill the gaps; marshrutkas are cheap but cramped and don't always run to a fixed timetable. Taxis are widely available and inexpensive by Western European standards, and ride-hailing apps work reliably. The Old Town and historic center are compact enough to walk, though the hilly terrain means some routes climb steeply.

Most travelers arrive at Tbilisi International Airport [NEEDS: exact airport-to-city-center distance and typical transfer time], with a bus and taxis connecting it to the center.

Best time to visit Tbilisi

The best time to visit Tbilisi is spring (April–June) and autumn (September–October), when daytime temperatures sit around 15–25°C and the weather is generally settled. Tbilisi has a humid subtropical climate with four seasons. Winters are mild — January averages around 2–4°C — with occasional snow on the hills. Summers are hot: July and August regularly reach 35°C or above, and the Old Town offers little shade. Most rain falls in spring and early summer. If wine matters to you, the September–October grape harvest is the liveliest time in the surrounding regions.

Day trips from Tbilisi

Tbilisi's central location makes it a strong base for day trips.

Mtskheta, the ancient capital and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, sits 20 kilometers north at the meeting of the Mtkvari and Aragvi rivers. The Jvari Monastery above the town and the Svetitskhoveli Cathedral within it are among Georgia's most important historic sites, and Mtskheta fits comfortably into a half day.

Kakheti, the main wine region, lies roughly 80–120 kilometers east, with the towns of Telavi, Sighnaghi, and Kvareli reachable in under two hours by road. This is where the highest concentration of visitable wineries sits, along with the Alaverdi Cathedral and the Bodbe Convent.

Kazbegi (Stepantsminda) in the Greater Caucasus is around 150 kilometers north via the Georgian Military Highway, built around the Gergeti Trinity Church above the village at over 2,000 meters. It's one of the most visited places in Georgia and can be done as a long day trip from Tbilisi, though staying overnight gives a much better experience and avoids the round trip in one day.

How Tbilisi fits into a Georgia itinerary

Almost every Georgia itinerary starts in Tbilisi. A typical first trip uses the city for two to three days, then branches out: east to Kakheti for wine, north to Kazbegi (Stepantsminda) for the high Caucasus, and — on longer trips — further west to Svaneti's mountain towers or the Black Sea coast. Because the road network radiates from the capital, Tbilisi works well either as a fixed base for day trips or as the first leg of a one-way route across the country.

Who Tbilisi suits

Tbilisi suits travelers who like cities with depth — history, food, wine, and neighborhoods to wander — and who don't mind some rough edges alongside the restored parts. If you're mainly after mountains and hiking, you'll want to treat Tbilisi as a short stop and spend your time in Kazbegi, Svaneti, or Tusheti instead. Two to three days is usually the right balance.

A brief history of Tbilisi

The Tbilisi area has been settled since at least the Bronze Age, but the city's traditional founding is credited to King Vakhtang Gorgasali in the 5th century AD. By Georgian chronicles, he found the site's hot springs while hunting and chose it for both its strategic position and its geothermal water; his son Dachi moved the capital from Mtskheta to Tbilisi in the early 6th century. The name comes from the Georgian tbili, "warm," after the sulfur springs that still flow beneath the city.

Sitting on trade routes between Europe and Asia made Tbilisi one of the most contested cities in the region. It was sacked by the Arab general Marwan ibn Muhammad in the 8th century, held under Arab rule for centuries, and later taken by the Seljuk Turks. The city peaked politically and culturally under King David the Builder in the early 12th century and Queen Tamar in the late 12th and early 13th centuries — the high point of medieval Georgia.

The Mongol invasions of the 13th century ended that era, and Tbilisi then changed hands repeatedly between Persian and Ottoman empires. The Persian Shah Agha Mohammad Khan burned much of the city in 1795. In 1801 Russia annexed the Kingdom of Kartli-Kakheti, and Tbilisi became the administrative center of the whole Caucasus under Russian rule — the period that shaped much of today's city center. Georgia declared independence in 1918, fell under Soviet rule in 1921, and restored independence in 1991. The early post-Soviet years were hard, with civil conflict and economic collapse; recovery picked up after the 2004 Rose Revolution, and the city has modernized steadily since.

faq.title: How many days do you need in Tbilisi?
faq.content: Two to three days covers Tbilisi itself — the Old Town, sulfur baths, Narikala Fortress, Rustaveli Avenue, and a couple of museums. Add a day for each day trip to Kakheti or Kazbegi.

faq.title: What is the best time to visit Tbilisi?
faq.content: Spring (April–June) and autumn (September–October), when temperatures are around 15–25°C. Summers can exceed 35°C with little shade in the Old Town; winters are mild with occasional snow. The September–October grape harvest is best for wine.

faq.title: Is Tbilisi worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes, especially for travelers who like history, food, and wine. It combines a walkable old quarter, working sulfur baths, strong restaurants, and easy access to wine country and the Caucasus mountains. Travelers focused only on hiking should keep their Tbilisi stop short.

faq.title: What are the best day trips from Tbilisi?
faq.content: Mtskheta (20 km, half a day), the Kakheti wine region (about 80–120 km east), and Kazbegi/Stepantsminda in the Greater Caucasus (about 150 km north). Kazbegi can be done in a long day but is better overnight.

faq.title: How do you get around Tbilisi?
faq.content: A two-line metro covers the center, with buses, marshrutkas, taxis, and ride-hailing apps filling the rest. The historic center is walkable, though hilly.

thingsToDoChiatura.heroTitle: Things to Do in Chiatura, Georgia: Cable Cars and the Katskhi Pillar
thingsToDoChiatura.content:
Chiatura is one of the most unusual destinations in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus. Set in a deep valley in the Imereti region of the west, the town is known for its dramatic canyon landscape, Soviet industrial heritage, and historic cable car system. This isn't a polished or traditional tourist destination, and that's the point: Chiatura offers a raw, authentic look at a lesser-seen side of Georgia, where history, industry, and daily life are still closely tied together. Visiting is less about classic sightseeing and more about taking in the atmosphere and the place's particular identity.

What is there to do in Chiatura?

The main things to do in Chiatura are riding the Soviet-era cable cars for the views over the canyon, seeing the nearby Katskhi Pillar (a medieval monastery atop a tall limestone rock), exploring the industrial townscape on foot, and visiting the cliffside Mghvimevi Monastery. It's usually done as a day trip from Kutaisi, about 1–1.5 hours away. Come for the atmosphere and the unusual landscape rather than comfort or polished attractions.

Ride the Chiatura cable cars

The cable car system is one of the main reasons to visit Chiatura. Originally built in the Soviet period to move workers across the steep valley, the cars became an essential part of daily life. Today some lines have been replaced with upgraded cabins while others remain closer to their original form, and riding them is both practical transport and a piece of living history. From above you get a clear view of the town's layout — houses built into the cliffs, industrial sites, and the narrow valley below — one of the most distinctive perspectives in Georgia. One practical note: operating hours and which lines run can be irregular, so check locally before building your day around them [NEEDS: confirm which cable car lines currently operate, their hours, and fares].

Visit the Katskhi Pillar

Just outside Chiatura, the Katskhi Pillar is one of the most striking natural and religious landmarks in the country: a tall limestone monolith with a small medieval monastery on its flat summit. The view from below is impressive on its own — the sheer rock face with the tiny church at the top. Summit access is restricted because it's an active monastic site, so most visitors experience it from the base [NEEDS: confirm current summit-access rules and any restrictions]. The combination of natural formation and human construction makes it one of the most photographed sites in the region.

Explore Chiatura's industrial landscape

Chiatura grew as a mining town, and that identity is still visible. The town is built into a narrow valley with steep rock walls on both sides, and industrial structures, cable lines, and residential buildings are all woven into that landscape. Walking through, you notice how the place grew around its function rather than following a typical urban grid, which is exactly what makes it visually and culturally different from other Georgian towns. For travelers interested in 20th-century history and Soviet architecture, this is the main draw.

Walk through the local neighborhoods

Exploring Chiatura on foot gives a better feel for daily life here. You'll pass Soviet-era residential blocks, small shops and local cafés, and streets that follow the natural shape of the valley. It's not a place designed for tourism, and that's what makes it interesting — the experience feels direct and uncurated. This is a functioning town where people live and work, not a preserved exhibit, which gives the Soviet-era surroundings more weight than any museum display.

Visit Mghvimevi Monastery

Near the center of Chiatura, Mghvimevi Monastery is partly built into the rock. The entrance, set into a cliff face, makes a strong first impression, and inside the monastery is simple but atmospheric. It's a small site, but it adds a cultural and religious element that balances the industrial side of the town. The approach involves a steep climb up steps cut into the cliff, rewarded with good views over the river and canyon.

Combine Chiatura with Kutaisi and western Georgia

Chiatura is usually visited as a day trip from Kutaisi, about 1–1.5 hours away by road. It pairs naturally with the Katskhi Pillar and the surrounding villages and countryside, which makes it an easy addition to a western Georgia itinerary. Most travelers fold both Chiatura and Katskhi into a single day out from Kutaisi.

Why Chiatura is worth visiting

Chiatura offers something most destinations don't. It isn't about comfort or polished experiences — it's about authenticity: Soviet industrial history, a unique urban landscape, everyday life in a non-touristic environment, and dramatic natural surroundings. It suits travelers who want to see beyond the standard routes and don't mind that the reward is atmosphere and character rather than comfort or classic sights.

faq.title: What are the main things to do in Chiatura?
faq.content: Ride the Soviet-era cable cars for the canyon views, see the nearby Katskhi Pillar, explore the industrial townscape on foot, and visit the cliffside Mghvimevi Monastery. It's usually done as a day trip from Kutaisi.

faq.title: Can you ride the Chiatura cable cars?
faq.content: Yes. Some lines have been restored with upgraded cabins while others remain closer to their original form, and they work as both local transport and a tourist experience. Hours and which lines run can be irregular, so check locally first.

faq.title: Can you climb the Katskhi Pillar?
faq.content: Generally no. The summit monastery is an active religious site with restricted access, so most visitors see it from the base — which is still dramatic. The pillar is a tall limestone monolith with a medieval church on top, just outside Chiatura.

faq.title: How do you get to Chiatura?
faq.content: Most easily from Kutaisi, about 1–1.5 hours by road, and usually visited as a day trip. It combines well with the Katskhi Pillar and the surrounding countryside in a western Georgia itinerary.

faq.title: Is Chiatura worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes, if you want something beyond the standard routes. It's not about comfort or polished sights but about authenticity — Soviet industrial history, an unusual canyon townscape, and everyday local life in a dramatic setting.

thingsToDoDmanisi.heroTitle: Things to Do in Dmanisi, Georgia: Prehistory and a Medieval Town
thingsToDoDmanisi.content:
Dmanisi is one of the most important historical and archaeological sites not only in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — but in the world. In the Kvemo Kartli region about 90 kilometers southwest of Tbilisi, it offers a very different experience from more traditional destinations. This isn't a place for casual sightseeing or city walks; it's about understanding deep history, both human and medieval. Dmanisi combines one of the earliest known human settlements outside Africa with the remains of a once-thriving medieval town. For travelers interested in archaeology, history, or unusual destinations, it's one of the most meaningful places to visit in Georgia.

What is there to do in Dmanisi?

The main things to do at Dmanisi are exploring the archaeological site where hominin remains dating back about 1.8 million years were found, walking the ruins of the medieval town, and visiting the early-medieval Dmanisi Sioni Cathedral — all on one plateau. Beyond the site, the open Kvemo Kartli landscape and nearby Bolnisi make it a strong half-day to full-day trip. Come for significance and atmosphere rather than a long list of attractions; this is a place to understand rather than simply photograph.

Explore the Dmanisi archaeological site

The Dmanisi archaeological site is internationally recognized for discoveries that changed the understanding of human evolution. Excavations here revealed human remains dating back roughly 1.8 million years — among the oldest known outside Africa — showing that early humans left Africa far earlier than previously believed. Visiting, you're not just seeing ruins but a place central to global scientific research. The excavation areas are partially visible, and information panels explain the significance of the finds. The site also includes later historical layers, which is what makes it unusual: prehistoric and medieval history in one location [NEEDS: confirm Dmanisi Museum-Reserve opening hours and ticket price].

Walk through the medieval city ruins

Beyond its prehistoric importance, Dmanisi was once a significant medieval town on trade routes connecting the Caucasus with the Middle East. Today you can walk through the remains of that settlement — defensive walls, residential structures, and street layouts — spread across the plateau, which gives a clear sense of how the town once functioned. Compared with reconstructed sites, Dmanisi feels raw and authentic, leaving you to imagine life here centuries ago rather than being shown a polished version of it.

Visit Dmanisi Sioni Cathedral

Within the complex, the Dmanisi Sioni Cathedral dates to the early medieval period and is one of the oldest surviving Christian structures in the region. The architecture is simple and reflects early Georgian church design, and inside the atmosphere is quiet and minimal, without the elaborate decoration of later churches. The cathedral adds a spiritual and cultural dimension that balances the scientific weight of the archaeological site. It remains a religious site, so dress modestly.

Enjoy the landscape and setting

Dmanisi sits on a plateau at the confluence of two rivers, surrounded by open landscapes and rolling hills. The setting is calm and spacious, with wide views across the valley. Compared with Georgia's mountain regions the landscape is more open and less dramatic, but still appealing in its own austere way, and it reinforces the sense of stepping into a place inhabited for thousands of years. One practical note: the plateau is exposed with little shade, so it can be hot in summer — visit in the morning in July and August.

Combine Dmanisi with Bolnisi and Kvemo Kartli

Dmanisi works best combined with nearby destinations such as Bolnisi and other parts of Kvemo Kartli. The region offers early Christian monuments, rural landscapes, small family wineries, and less-visited cultural sites, which makes it ideal for travelers exploring beyond the main tourist routes. Bolnisi, about 40 kilometers northeast, pairs especially well — its 5th-century cathedral carries some of the earliest known written Georgian.

Why Dmanisi is worth visiting

Dmanisi isn't about quantity but about significance. It offers one of the oldest human archaeological sites outside Africa, a rare combination of prehistoric and medieval history, quiet and uncrowded surroundings, and a genuinely educational experience. It suits travelers who want something distinctive and globally significant, and who value understanding a place over ticking off sights.

faq.title: What are the main things to do at Dmanisi?
faq.content: Explore the archaeological site where roughly 1.8-million-year-old hominin remains were found, walk the ruins of the medieval town, and visit the early-medieval Dmanisi Sioni Cathedral — all on one plateau. The open Kvemo Kartli landscape and nearby Bolnisi round out a half- to full-day trip.

faq.title: Why is the Dmanisi archaeological site significant?
faq.content: It produced human remains dating back about 1.8 million years, among the oldest known outside Africa, which showed early humans left Africa far earlier than previously believed. The site is internationally important to the study of human evolution.

faq.title: Can you see the medieval town at Dmanisi?
faq.content: Yes. Marked areas of the plateau hold the remains of a once-significant medieval trading town — defensive walls, residential structures, and street layouts — alongside the early-medieval Dmanisi Sioni Cathedral.

faq.title: What can you combine with Dmanisi?
faq.content: Bolnisi, about 40 km northeast, with its 5th-century cathedral and early Georgian inscriptions, plus other Kvemo Kartli sites, rural landscapes, and small wineries — ideal for travelers exploring beyond the main routes.

faq.title: Is Dmanisi worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes, especially for those interested in archaeology and history. It's not about a long list of attractions but about significance — one of the oldest human sites outside Africa, combined with medieval ruins, in quiet, uncrowded surroundings.

thingsToDoTbilisi.heroTitle: Things to Do in Tbilisi, Georgia: A Local Guide to the Capital
thingsToDoTbilisi.content:
Tbilisi is the capital of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus, not the US state — and the city where most trips to the country begin. The historic core is compact and walkable, so you can cover the main sights on foot over two to three days, with sulfur baths, old churches, museums, and one of the world's oldest wine cultures packed into a small area. Below is what's worth your time, how long each thing takes, and where the gap between the hype and the reality is widest.

What are the best things to do in Tbilisi?

The best things to do in Tbilisi are walking the Old Town (Dzveli Tbilisi), soaking in the Abanotubani sulfur baths, going up to Narikala Fortress for the city view, seeing Sameba Cathedral and the cluster of churches, a mosque and a synagogue in the historic center, visiting the Georgian National Museum on Rustaveli Avenue, and eating and drinking through the city's restaurants and wine bars. Most of these sit within walking distance of each other, which is why two to three days is enough for a first visit.

Walk the Old Town (Dzveli Tbilisi)

Walking the Old Town is the first thing most visitors do in Tbilisi, and it's the right call. The lanes wind without much logic between houses whose carved wooden balconies hang out over the street, often draped with vines or laundry. Many buildings are half-restored and half-crumbling — plaster peeling to show older layers of paint, balconies leaning at angles. That unevenness is honest: large parts of the Old Town are lived-in and worn rather than polished for tourists, and a few streets back from the main routes you'll see real disrepair.

Give it half a day on foot. Wear shoes with grip — the cobbles are uneven and several routes climb.

Bathe in the Abanotubani sulfur baths

Bathing in the Abanotubani sulfur baths is the most distinctly Tbilisi thing you can do. The domed brick bathhouses sit below Narikala Fortress and are fed by natural hot springs; they've operated in some form since the city's earliest days. The water surfaces at roughly 37–47°C, smells faintly of sulfur, and is genuinely hot. You can pay for a private room by the hour or use the public sections [NEEDS: current price ranges for private rooms vs public entry and typical session length].

Set expectations: these are working bathhouses, not luxury spas. The rooms are simple and the sulfur smell is part of the deal. An optional kisi (scrub-down) is offered at most baths and is worth trying once.

Go up to Narikala Fortress

Going up to Narikala Fortress gives you the clearest overview of how Tbilisi sits in its valley — the Old Town below, the Mtkvari River winding through, the newer districts to the north, and Mtatsminda hill rising behind. The fortress dates to the 4th century, was expanded under Arab and Georgian rule, and was largely wrecked when a gunpowder store inside exploded in 1827. What's left is a ruin you can walk around rather than an intact castle.

A cable car runs up from the Old Town side [NEEDS: confirm cable car lower station name, fare, and operating hours]. You can also walk up through the Old Town, which is steep but free. Budget about an hour including the view.

See Sameba Cathedral and the Old Town's religious sites

Sameba Cathedral — formally the Holy Trinity Cathedral — is the most prominent religious building in modern Tbilisi. It was completed in 2004, stands on Elia Hill in the Avlabari district, and is one of the largest Orthodox cathedrals in the world by floor area; its golden dome rises about 84 meters [NEEDS: confirm 84 m dome height] and is visible across the city. It is the seat of the head of the Georgian Orthodox Church. Dress modestly — shoulders and knees covered, headscarves available for women at the entrance.

What's more interesting is the religious mix in the historic center. Within a short walk you can find medieval Georgian Orthodox churches (including the 6th-century Anchiskhati Basilica, the oldest in the city, and Metekhi Church on its cliff), the Armenian Apostolic cathedral, a mosque that has served Tbilisi's Muslim community since the city's Persian-influenced period, and a working synagogue. This isn't a curated diversity showcase — it's the leftover of Tbilisi's long history as a trading crossroads where these communities lived side by side.

Visit the Georgian National Museum

Visiting the Georgian National Museum on Rustaveli Avenue is the fastest way to make sense of everything else you'll see in Georgia. It holds prehistoric gold, medieval manuscripts, and a Soviet occupation exhibition that gives essential 20th-century context. The same complex includes the Dmanisi hominid fossils — among the earliest human-ancestor remains found outside Africa. Allow one to two hours.

Rustaveli Avenue itself, named after the 12th-century poet Shota Rustaveli, is the main 19th-century boulevard and worth a walk for the architecture, the Rustaveli Theatre, and the Parliament building [NEEDS: confirm current use/status of the Rustaveli Parliament building]. Nearby Freedom Square has the gilded St. George statue where a Lenin statue once stood. Both are places to walk through rather than linger.

Eat your way through Georgian food

Eating is one of the main things to do in Tbilisi, because in Georgia a shared meal is a core part of how people connect. The traditional feast, or supra, is led by a tamada (toastmaster) and runs through a long sequence of dishes and toasts. You don't need a formal supra to get the idea — a normal restaurant dinner shows the spirit.

Two dishes to start with: khinkali, the large pleated soup dumplings filled with spiced meat, eaten by hand — hold by the thick stem, bite a small hole, drink the broth first, then eat the dumpling and leave the knot on your plate (locals often count the knots to see how many you've had). And khachapuri, cheese-filled bread; the Adjarian version is a boat of bread filled with molten cheese, topped with a raw egg and butter you stir in at the table. Beyond those, expect walnut-based sauces, herb-heavy salads, slow stews, and a strong range of vegetable and bean dishes thanks to the Orthodox fasting tradition — useful if you're vegetarian. One caveat: restaurants right on the main Old Town tourist streets are hit-or-miss; the food is usually better a few streets off the main drag.

Taste Georgian wine in the city's wine bars

Tasting wine is worth building into your time in Tbilisi even if you don't visit the vineyards. Georgia is widely considered one of the oldest wine-producing regions in the world, with archaeological evidence of winemaking going back roughly 8,000 years. The traditional method ferments grapes — skins, seeds, and all — in buried clay vessels called qvevri, which produces the amber or "orange" wines Georgia is now known for. They have a tannic grip that can surprise people used to standard white wine.

Tbilisi's wine bars usually pour qvevri styles and modern winery wines side by side, so you can compare. Ask for Rkatsiteli (the main white grape, grown in Kakheti) and Saperavi (the dark grape behind the country's best-known reds). It's also a good way to decide whether a full wine tour in Kakheti is for you before committing a day to it.

Spend an evening in Vera and Vake

For an evening away from the historic sites, the Vera and Vake districts northwest of the center have the city's denser café and bar culture, popular with locals as much as visitors. This is also where much of Tbilisi's newer restaurant and arts scene sits. It's residential and low on landmarks — the point is the atmosphere and the food, not sightseeing.

Go up Mtatsminda for the view

Going up Mtatsminda, the forested hill on the west side of the city, gives you a different angle on Tbilisi from the one at Narikala. There's a viewpoint and park at the top [NEEDS: confirm Mtatsminda funicular vs road access, fare, and hours]. Best in late afternoon, when the light is good and the city below starts to switch on.

Day trips you can add from Tbilisi

If you have extra days, Tbilisi is a strong base for short trips. Mtskheta, the ancient capital and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is about 20 km north where two rivers meet and is easily a half-day. Kakheti, the main wine region, is roughly 80–120 km east (under two hours by road) for vineyards and monasteries. Kazbegi (Stepantsminda) in the Greater Caucasus is about 150 km north via the Georgian Military Highway, built around the Gergeti Trinity Church above the village — doable as a long day trip, better with an overnight.

When to do all this

Spring (April–June) and autumn (September–October) are the most comfortable times for walking the city, with temperatures around 15–25°C. Summers get hot — July and August often top 35°C, and the Old Town has little shade, so plan baths and museums for midday and walking for mornings and evenings. Winters are mild with occasional snow and noticeably quieter.

How long do you need?

Two to three days covers the main things to do in Tbilisi at a reasonable pace: a day for the Old Town, baths, and Narikala; a day for Rustaveli, the National Museum, and Sameba; and time around both for food and wine. Add a day for each day trip. The most common mistake is treating Tbilisi as a one-night stop on the way to the mountains — you can, but you'll miss most of what's here.

faq.title: What are the top things to do in Tbilisi?
faq.content: Walk the Old Town, soak in the Abanotubani sulfur baths, go up to Narikala Fortress for the view, see Sameba Cathedral and the historic center's churches, mosque and synagogue, visit the Georgian National Museum, and eat and drink through the city's restaurants and wine bars.

faq.title: Are the Tbilisi sulfur baths worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes, as a one-time experience. The Abanotubani baths are fed by natural hot springs at around 37–47°C and are a centuries-old part of city life. Expect simple, working bathhouses rather than luxury spas, and a faint sulfur smell.

faq.title: How many days do you need to see Tbilisi?
faq.content: Two to three days covers the main sights at a reasonable pace. Add a day for each day trip to Mtskheta, Kakheti, or Kazbegi.

faq.title: Can you visit Narikala Fortress for free?
faq.content: Yes — the fortress ruins are open to walk around at no charge. You can hike up through the Old Town for free, or take the paid cable car.

faq.title: What is the best time of year to visit Tbilisi?
faq.content: Spring (April–June) and autumn (September–October), when temperatures are around 15–25°C. Summers can exceed 35°C; winters are mild and quieter.

destinations.intro: Georgia packs an enormous amount into a small country — a walkable capital, ancient wine regions, Black Sea beaches, and high Caucasus mountains. Explore it by region, by city, or by the individual places worth planning a trip around. Our guides cover what to see, how long to stay, and honest local tips, with more added over time.

destinationsRegions.intro: Georgia divides into distinct regions, each with its own landscape, food, and history — from the wine cellars of the east to the high Caucasus peaks and the Black Sea coast. Use this overview to find the region that fits your trip; detailed regional guides are being added over time.

kakheti.name: Kakheti
kakheti.description: Georgia's eastern wine country — qvevri cellars, the hilltop town of Sighnaghi, and the Alazani Valley.

imereti.name: Imereti
imereti.description: A green central region around Kutaisi, with Gelati Monastery and the Prometheus and Sataplia caves.

adjara.name: Adjara

samtskhe-javakheti.name: Samtskhe-Javakheti

racha-lechkhumi.name: Racha-Lechkhumi

mtskheta-mtianeti.name: Mtskheta-Mtianeti

samegrelo.name: Samegrelo
samegrelo.description: Western lowlands with the Martvili and Okatse canyons and a distinct Mingrelian cuisine.

svaneti.name: Svaneti

guria.name: Guria

kvemo-kartli.name: Kvemo Kartli

tbilisi.name: Tbilisi

sighnaghi.name: Sighnaghi

stepantsminda.name: Stepantsminda (Kazbegi)
